steps.heading: What is Artificial Intelligence?
steps.body: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is the ability of a computer system to perform tasks that normally require human intelligence — like understanding language, recognizing patterns, making decisions, and solving problems.

steps.heading: AI is everywhere
steps.body: AI powers the recommendations on Netflix, spam filters in your email, voice assistants like Siri and Alexa, and the autocomplete on your keyboard. It's not science fiction — it's part of everyday life.

steps.question: Which of these is an example of AI?
options: A spreadsheet calculating a sum
A thermostat turning on the heat
A system that recommends movies based on your history
A clock showing the current time

title: What is a Large Language Model?

steps.heading: What is a Large Language Model?
steps.body: A Large Language Model (LLM) is a type of AI trained on massive amounts of text. It learns to predict what words come next in a sentence — and through that process it develops a deep understanding of language, facts, and reasoning.

steps.heading: How does it generate text?
steps.body: When you send a message to an LLM like ChatGPT, it reads your input and generates a response one token (word fragment) at a time — always choosing the most likely next token based on everything it has learned.

steps.question: What does an LLM primarily learn to do during training?
options: Memorize an encyclopedia
Predict the next word in a sequence
Search the internet in real time
Execute code instructions

steps.heading: What is a prompt?
steps.body: A prompt is the text you send to an AI model. It's your way of communicating what you want. The quality of the output depends heavily on the quality of the input — this is where prompt engineering comes in.

steps.heading: Garbage in, garbage out
steps.body: Vague prompts produce vague results. Specific, well-structured prompts produce specific, useful results. Learning to write good prompts is one of the most valuable skills in the AI era.

steps.question: What is a 'prompt' when working with an AI model?
options: A reminder notification from an app
The text input you send to the model
A predefined answer stored in the model
A setting in the model configuration

steps.heading: The anatomy of a good prompt
steps.body: A well-structured prompt usually contains three parts: a role or context ('You are a senior developer...'), clear instructions ('Review this code and identify bugs'), and the input ('Here is the code: ...'). Together they guide the model precisely.

steps.heading: Context is everything
steps.body: The more relevant context you provide, the better the output. Telling the model who it is, what format you want, and what constraints apply will dramatically improve your results.

steps.question: Which prompt is likely to produce the best result?
options: Fix my code
You are a senior JavaScript developer. Review the following function for bugs and explain each issue: [code]
Help me
JavaScript function review needed

steps.heading: Prompts in practice
steps.body: Good prompts are direct, specific, and give the model a role. Example: 'You are a technical writer. Summarize the following documentation in 3 bullet points for a junior developer.' This sets expectations for tone, format, and audience.

steps.heading: Iteration is key
steps.body: Don't expect perfection on the first try. Prompting is iterative — you send a prompt, evaluate the response, and refine. Each iteration teaches you what the model responds to best.

steps.question: What is the recommended approach when a prompt doesn't give the result you want?
options: Switch to a different AI tool
Accept the result as-is
Refine and iterate on the prompt
Report it as a bug
